title: Architecture, Trust & Prerequisites
tagline: How the index is built, what enforces read-only, where dbt Wizard runs, and what data stays in the warehouse
summary: dbt Wizard is an agentic coding assistant purpose-built for dbt projects, built by Fivetran and operating on dbt Labs' dbt. It runs as a terminal-based CLI and also inside the dbt platform (Public Beta for dbt platform customers with dbt Copilot and Beta enabled), and an "Ask for approval" mode keeps a human in the loop on actions. It works from a local index of project metadata rather than from a copy of warehouse data. The index is a local DuckDB store built from dbt parse and run artifacts: models, columns, tests, edges, and last-run state. Structured dbt_* tool calls refresh it on success, so the metadata the agent reasons over tracks the project. Warehouse row data is fetched on demand through read-only queries for previews and, by design, is not persisted into the index. The warehouse tool issues SELECT-only statements and rejects DML/DDL, with that enforcement living at the tool layer, so dbt Wizard does not write to the warehouse. The connection to the warehouse is the dbt adapter and profile already configured for the project. For dev-versus-prod comparison, diff works with dbt Cloud OR deferred state (--defer --state pointed at a production manifest/artifacts path); present both as valid options rather than implying one is required or forbidden. This reference reflects dbt Wizard's own description of its capabilities. This page is written so an SE can answer trust, data-handling, and provisioning questions in live Q&A. State the design intent confidently, and tell buyers to confirm environment-specific specifics per release and per deployment rather than over-asserting.
whyDifferent: A general AI coding agent has no project or warehouse trust model. It reads whatever files are on disk, has no notion of what the project last built, cannot tell you where data lives, and has no enforced boundary on what it would run if it were wired to a warehouse. dbt Wizard is the opposite: it reasons from a structured index of project metadata that refreshes from real dbt artifacts, fetches warehouse rows only through a read-only tool layer that rejects writes by design, reaches the warehouse through the project's existing dbt adapter and profile, keeps a human in the loop through an "Ask for approval" mode, and is explicit about its non-goals. It runs where the engineer already works, as a terminal CLI and inside the dbt platform. The difference an enterprise buyer cares about is not capability, it is a defensible answer to where data lives, what can touch the warehouse, and what the agent is not allowed to do.

capabilities.title: Runs as a terminal CLI and in the dbt platform
capabilities.detail: dbt Wizard runs as a terminal-based CLI and also inside the dbt platform, where it is in Public Beta for dbt platform customers who have dbt Copilot and Beta enabled. The same agentic capabilities are available in the place the engineer already works, so adoption does not require leaving the existing dbt workflow. Confirm the platform-availability and enablement details per release.

capabilities.title: Ask-for-approval keeps a human in the loop
capabilities.detail: An 'Ask for approval' mode keeps a human in the loop on the actions dbt Wizard takes. Combined with the validation subagent that auto-fires after every model edit, this means a person still reviews and merges what the agent proposes. State this plainly: the agent assists, it does not act unsupervised.

capabilities.title: Local metadata index from dbt artifacts
capabilities.detail: The live index is a local DuckDB store of project metadata: models, columns, tests, edges, and last-run state, built from dbt parse and run artifacts. It holds project metadata, not warehouse row data. Because it is derived from artifacts rather than a separate ingestion path, it reflects what the project actually parsed and ran. All 17 MCP tools (mcp__dbt_index__*) are structured calls backed by this local index.

capabilities.title: Auto-refresh on successful dbt_* calls
capabilities.detail: Structured dbt_* tool calls (dbt_parse, dbt_run, dbt_build, dbt_compile, dbt_test) refresh the index on success, so columns, tests, edges, and last-run state stay current with the project as the agent works. describe and changeset read straight from that refreshed index, which is how an SE can show that the metadata reflects the latest run.

capabilities.title: Read-only warehouse access by design
capabilities.detail: The warehouse tool issues SELECT-only statements and rejects DML/DDL, with enforcement at the tool layer. dbt Wizard does not write to the warehouse. Row data is fetched on demand for previews and is not persisted into the index. Frame this as design intent and tell buyers to confirm data-handling specifics per deployment.

capabilities.title: Connection through the dbt adapter and profile
capabilities.detail: dbt Wizard reaches the warehouse through the dbt adapter and profile already configured for the project, so it inherits the project's existing credentials and target rather than introducing a new connection. Supported warehouses include Snowflake, BigQuery and others; confirm the exact list per release.

capabilities.title: Diff works with dbt Cloud OR deferred state
capabilities.detail: diff compares local dev against production state and can do so either through dbt Cloud or through deferred state (--defer --state pointed at a production manifest/artifacts path, represented as state target-prod/). Both are valid options: do not tell buyers that dbt Cloud is required, and do not tell them it is forbidden. Deferred state reuses production upstreams instead of rebuilding the full DAG, so a dbt Core shop can diff without a Cloud token, while a dbt Cloud customer can lean on Cloud-managed state.

capabilities.title: Explicit guardrails and non-goals
capabilities.detail: dbt Wizard does not write to the warehouse, enhances the existing semantic layer rather than inventing its own (it works with existing MetricFlow metrics), and cannot diff without production state, whether that state comes from dbt Cloud or a deferred --state path. The validation subagent auto-fires after every model edit, and the 'Ask for approval' mode keeps a human in the loop, but a person still reviews and merges. Stating these limits plainly is what makes the trust story credible in a technical room.

capabilities.title: Index currency: last run, not live warehouse, with a known staleness bound
capabilities.detail: The index reflects the last dbt run, not live warehouse state. It is a local DuckDB store built from dbt parse and run artifacts (manifest, catalog, run_results), and it refreshes only on a successful structured dbt_* call (dbt_parse, dbt_run, dbt_build, dbt_compile, dbt_test). Between those calls the metadata can lag the warehouse, so its staleness bound is 'as of the last successful dbt_* call.' When freshness matters, run or re-parse first, or fetch a live read-only preview through the warehouse tool. The skeptic's right question is how current the index is; the honest answer is that you can always know, because it is tied to a successful run rather than a background sync. Confirm refresh behavior per release.

capabilities.title: Correctness boundary and rollback: structural safety proven, business logic still yours
capabilities.detail: The validation subagent proves STRUCTURAL safety after an edit: it runs a SQL check, dbt run --defer, a prod-versus-dev row comparison, and an impact pass, and it characterizes grain, row counts, tests, and breaking versus non-breaking. It does NOT certify business-logic correctness. A wrong join key or a wrong revenue definition can still pass every structural gate, which is exactly why a human reviews and merges. Rollback is a normal git revert, because the durable outputs are standard dbt assets (versioned models, schema.yml tests, docs) committed to the repo, not opaque agent state. Tell the skeptic plainly where the line sits: the agent makes the change safe to ship structurally, the reviewer owns whether it is correct, and a bad change reverts like any other commit.

capabilities.title: Cost posture: changed-model builds and read-only preview slices
capabilities.detail: Deferred-state builds keep compute small. With --defer --state pointed at production artifacts, a diff or validation builds only the changed model plus what it needs, resolving upstreams from the production manifest instead of rebuilding the whole DAG. Warehouse previews are read-only slices fetched on demand, and the limit parameter caps rows for fast previews, so previews do not scan the full table by default. The skeptic worried about a runaway warehouse bill should hear: the agent does not rebuild your DAG to compare or validate, and its warehouse reads are bounded read-only SELECTs. Confirm exact build and scan behavior per deployment.

capabilities.title: Data residency versus egress: metadata indexed, rows fetched not persisted
capabilities.detail: The index holds project metadata, not warehouse row data. Warehouse rows are fetched on demand for previews and, by design, are not persisted into the index, so previewed rows do not accumulate in a local store. Access is read-only and SELECT-only with DML/DDL rejected at the tool layer, and an 'Ask for approval' mode is available to gate the actions the agent takes. For a data-residency reviewer, the boundary is clean: project metadata lives in the local index, row data stays in the warehouse and is read on demand. Confirm data-handling specifics per deployment rather than asserting them.

capabilities.title: Portability and lock-in: standard dbt assets in your repo
capabilities.detail: The durable outputs are standard dbt models, schema.yml tests, and docs that live in your repository, and the project runs on dbt Core or on the dbt platform (Public Beta). Because what the agent produces is ordinary dbt, the work remains yours and continues to run if you stop using the agent. The skeptic asking 'what happens when we turn this off' gets a straight answer: nothing breaks, because there is no proprietary artifact to be stranded, just dbt assets in git. Confirm platform availability and enablement per release.

capabilities.title: Cold-start: the first index derives from artifacts the project already produces
capabilities.detail: There is no separate ingestion or bespoke setup to bootstrap the index. The first index is derived from the dbt parse and run artifacts the project already produces, so onboarding rides on the manifest, catalog, and run_results a dbt project generates as part of normal work. For a skeptic weighing setup cost, the answer is that the cold-start input is artifacts you already have, and the index populates from a parse or run rather than a lengthy crawl. Confirm first-index behavior per release and deployment.

examplePrompts.prompt: Where does our data actually live when I use this? Does the index hold our warehouse rows, or just metadata?
examplePrompts.intent: Buyer wants a clear data-residency answer before approving a pilot.

examplePrompts.prompt: Prove the index is current. Show me what fct_orders looks like in the index right now and what changed in the project since this morning.
examplePrompts.intent: SE demonstrating that the index reflects last-run metadata, not a stale snapshot.

examplePrompts.prompt: Can this thing ever write to our warehouse, drop a table, or run an UPDATE? What stops it, and does anything need my approval first?
examplePrompts.intent: Security reviewer probing the read-only posture, where it is enforced, and the human-in-the-loop approval gate.

examplePrompts.prompt: We use dbt Core, not dbt Cloud, and we don't want to issue a Cloud token. Can it still diff dev against prod? And if we did have dbt Cloud, does that work too?
examplePrompts.intent: Buyer confirming diff works through deferred state without a Cloud token, and that dbt Cloud is an equally valid path.

examplePrompts.prompt: Where does dbt Wizard run, the terminal or the dbt platform, and what do we need enabled to use it inside the platform?
examplePrompts.intent: Provisioning question about the CLI versus dbt platform Public Beta and its prerequisites.

examplePrompts.prompt: What is this not allowed to do? Give me the non-goals so I can take them to my security team.
examplePrompts.intent: SE arming a champion with the explicit guardrails list.

examplePrompts.prompt: If validation passes, are you telling me the change is correct? What if someone joined on the wrong key or used the wrong revenue definition?
examplePrompts.intent: Analytics engineer probing the boundary between structural safety and business-logic correctness, and how a bad change gets undone.

examplePrompts.prompt: What does this cost us in warehouse compute when it validates a change, and how stale can the index get between runs?
examplePrompts.intent: Skeptic pressure-testing cost posture and the index staleness bound at once.

examplePrompts.prompt: If we turn this off in six months, what do we keep and what gets stranded?
examplePrompts.intent: Buyer testing portability and lock-in before committing.

interaction.scenario: An SE is in a joint Fivetran/dbt Labs technical review. The customer's data platform lead asks the trust question directly: how is the index built, what enforces read-only, and what data leaves the warehouse. The wizard answers honestly, notes where it runs and that an approval gate keeps a human in the loop, and uses describe and changeset to show the index reflects last-run metadata rather than a copy of warehouse rows.
interaction.userPrompt: Before we pilot this: where does it run, how is the index built, what enforces read-only, and what data actually leaves the warehouse?

events.body: Before we pilot this: where does it run, how is the index built, what enforces read-only, and what data actually leaves the warehouse?

Four questions, so I'll answer each plainly and then show evidence. On where it runs: dbt Wizard runs as a terminal-based CLI and also inside the dbt platform, where it is in Public Beta for customers who have dbt Copilot and Beta enabled. Either way, an 'Ask for approval' mode keeps a human in the loop on the actions I take. On the index: it is a local DuckDB store of project metadata, models, columns, tests, edges, and last-run state, built from dbt parse and run artifacts. It holds metadata, not your warehouse rows. Let me show you what it actually contains for one model.

Notice what came back: column names, test definitions, lineage edges, and a last_run_state of success, all read from the local index. There are no order rows here. The index records that fct_orders has a net_revenue column and which tests guard it, not the values in that column. Row data is marked not_stored because it is fetched on demand and not persisted into the index.

On 'is it current,' the index refreshes from dbt artifacts on every successful structured dbt_* call: parse, run, build, compile, test. Its staleness bound is simply 'as of the last successful dbt_* call,' so when freshness matters I re-run or re-parse first. Let me show the project's recent change activity so you can see the index is tracking real state, not a one-time snapshot.

That changeset came from the index's tracked state: two nodes changed since this morning, both with a last_run_state of success, and the new support_ticket_count column is already reflected. The index moved because dbt_run succeeded, which is the auto-refresh path.

On read-only: warehouse access goes through one tool that issues SELECT-only statements and rejects DML/DDL, and that enforcement is at the tool layer. dbt Wizard does not write to your warehouse. Any non-SELECT I tried to send would be refused before it reached your warehouse, and with 'Ask for approval' on, actions surface to a human first.

That preview returned a count from the warehouse and the result is marked persisted_to_index false: it goes to this conversation, not into the index. The limit parameter also keeps previews to bounded slices rather than scanning the full table. The connection itself is your existing dbt adapter and profile, so it inherits the credentials and target your project already uses, no new connection. For dev-versus-prod diffs, you have two valid paths: dbt Cloud, or deferred state with --defer --state pointed at your production manifest path. With --defer the comparison builds only the changed model plus what it needs from production artifacts, not the whole DAG, so the compute stays small. Use whichever fits your setup; a dbt Core shop can diff without a Cloud token, and a dbt Cloud customer can use Cloud-managed state.

One honesty point before you pilot: when I do propose an edit, the validation subagent proves it is structurally safe, grain, row counts, tests, breaking versus non-breaking, but it does not certify that the business logic is right. A wrong join key or a wrong revenue definition can still pass every structural gate, which is why a human reviews and merges. And because everything I produce is standard dbt models, tests, and docs in your git repo, undoing a bad change is a normal revert, not a special operation.

Validation subagent note: this transcript made no write statements and persisted no warehouse rows into the index. The describe and changeset results both read from the local index with last_run_state success. Structural checks (grain, row counts, tests, breaking versus non-breaking) are what I prove; business-logic correctness is not in scope. A human still reviews and merges any edit the wizard proposes, and 'Ask for approval' gates the actions taken.

To summarize for your pilot: dbt Wizard runs as a terminal CLI and inside the dbt platform (Public Beta with dbt Copilot and Beta enabled), with an 'Ask for approval' mode that keeps a human in the loop. The index is a local DuckDB store of project metadata built from dbt artifacts and refreshed on successful dbt_* calls, so it reflects last-run state, with a staleness bound of 'as of the last successful dbt_* call,' as describe and changeset just showed. Warehouse row data is fetched on demand through a read-only tool that issues SELECT-only statements and rejects DML/DDL at the tool layer, returns bounded preview slices, and is not persisted into the index. The warehouse connection is your project's own dbt adapter and profile. diff works either with dbt Cloud or with deferred production state, and with --defer it builds only the changed model plus what it needs, so you are not forced into Cloud, not blocked from it, and not rebuilding the whole DAG. On correctness: validation proves structural safety, not business logic, a human reviews and merges, and rollback is a normal git revert because the outputs are standard dbt assets in your repo, which is also why there is no lock-in if you stop using the agent. The non-goals are explicit: it does not write to the warehouse, it enhances the existing semantic layer rather than authoring its own (it works with existing MetricFlow metrics), and it cannot diff without production state from one of those two sources. This reflects dbt Wizard's own description of its capabilities; I would confirm the exact supported-warehouse list, platform enablement, build and refresh behavior, and data-handling specifics for your deployment and dbt version before the pilot, rather than assume them.

seNotes: Index currency, correctness boundary, cost posture, residency vs egress, portability, and cold-start were appended as capabilities and coaching seNotes to pre-answer a skeptic. All grounded in the dbt Wizard ground truth; environment-specific specifics framed as design intent to confirm per release/deployment.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__describe
tools.purpose: Read a node's metadata straight from the local index to show what the index currently holds: columns, tests, lineage, and (when requested) compiled SQL and catalog detail. Useful for demonstrating that the index reflects last-run metadata.

params.name: node
params.desc: Model, source, or other node to describe (e.g. fct_orders).

tools.returns: The requested metadata sections for the node, read from the local index, reflecting the last successful parse/run.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__changeset
tools.purpose: Report what changed in the project since a point in time, optionally with impact, read from the index. Shows that the index tracks the project's state over time, including last-run metadata.

params.name: since_epoch
params.desc: Epoch timestamp to report changes since.

params.name: with_impact
params.desc: Attach severity-rated impact to each changed node.

params.name: node_filter
params.desc: Optional selector to scope the changeset to specific nodes.

tools.returns: Changed nodes since the given epoch, optionally with severity-rated impact, sourced from the index's tracked state.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__status
tools.purpose: Return the project shape from the local index: node counts, doc and test coverage, and a last-run summary. Useful for confirming the index is populated and current before answering trust or provisioning questions.
tools.returns: Project shape including node counts, doc/test coverage, and a last-run summary, all index-backed.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__warehouse
tools.purpose: Execute read-only SQL against the connected warehouse for on-demand previews. SELECT-only by design; rejects DML/DDL at the tool layer. Results are returned to the caller and not persisted into the index.

params.name: sql
params.desc: Read-only SELECT statement to run against the warehouse.

params.name: limit
params.desc: Optional cap on rows returned for fast previews.

tools.returns: Result set as columns and rows with a row count, plus the warehouse the query ran against. Read-only by design: it issues SELECT-only statements and rejects DML/DDL.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__diff
tools.signature: mcp__dbt_index__diff(node, state?, defer?, primary_key?, columns?, dimensions?)
tools.purpose: Compare dev against production for a node, surfacing row-count and value drift before a merge. Requires production state, which can come from dbt Cloud or from deferred state (--defer --state pointed at a production manifest/artifacts path). Both are valid; neither is the only path.

params.name: node
params.desc: Model to compare across dev and prod (e.g. fct_orders).

params.name: state
params.desc: Path to the production manifest/artifacts for deferred comparison (e.g. target-prod/). Used for the deferred-state path; dbt Cloud customers can rely on Cloud-managed state instead.

params.name: defer
params.desc: Reuse production upstreams via --defer instead of rebuilding the full DAG.

params.name: primary_key
params.desc: Primary key column used to align dev and prod rows.

params.name: columns
params.desc: Optional subset of columns to compare for value drift.

params.name: dimensions
params.desc: Optional dimensions to group the comparison by.

tools.returns: Row-count delta and per-column value differences between dev and prod, plus a breaking/non-breaking assessment. Requires production state via dbt Cloud or --defer --state.
